---
title: "Other Software Repository Setup"
subtitle: "For Stata, SAS, other editors, and CLI agent users"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
---

Use this walkthrough if you are not using RStudio, Positron, or VS Code as your main editor.

# What You Need

You need:

- Git installed.
- A terminal app.
- A folder where you keep course or project files.
- Optional: a CLI agent such as Codex CLI, Claude Code, Gemini CLI, or another approved tool.

# Windows Checklist

Before cloning the course repository on Windows, check these items.

## Git Is Installed

Open **PowerShell** and run:

```powershell
git --version
```

If Git is installed, you should see a version number.

If Windows says `git` is not recognized, install Git from:

<https://git-scm.com/downloads/win>

During installation, choose the option that allows Git to be used from the command line. This adds Git to your PATH.

## Git Is On Your PATH

After installing Git, close and reopen PowerShell, then run:

```powershell
git --version
```

If this still fails, restart your computer. If it still fails after restarting, Git is not on your PATH. [The second answer on this StackOverflow Post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/26620312/git-installing-git-in-path-with-github-client-for-windows) provides a good tutorial on how to fix the PATH issue.

## Choose A Directory For Course Materials

Pick a folder for the course repository. For example:

```text
C:\Users\yourname\Code
```

Avoid saving the course repository inside a synced or protected folder if possible, such as OneDrive Desktop or a restricted institutional folder.

# macOS Checklist

Open **Terminal** and run:

```bash
git --version
```

If Git is missing, macOS may prompt you to install Command Line Tools. Follow the prompt.

# Clone The Course Repository

Open a terminal and move to the folder where you want the course repository to live.

Windows example:

```powershell
cd C:\Users\yourname\Code
```

macOS/Linux example:

```bash
cd ~/Code
```

Then clone the repository:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/erikwestlund/ai-tools-2026.git
```

Move into the course folder:

```bash
cd ai-tools-2026
```

# Open The Course Website

Open `index.html` from your file browser.

This is the main course navigation page. It links to slides, modules, practice tasks, and assignments.

# Update The Course Repository

If you are not using R, update from the terminal:

```bash
git pull --ff-only
```

**Note:** The updater.R provides other benefits than pull, such as handling file conflicts, and scaffolding out practice examples.

If you are using R from any editor or terminal, you can instead run this from the course repository root:

```r
source("updater.R")
```

The updater does two things:

- pulls the latest course files,
- copies missing practice task files into `practice/work/` without overwriting your existing work.

# Where To Work

Course-owned folders may change during updates:

- `modules/`
- `slides/`
- `assignments/`
- `data/`
- `skills/`
- `practice/tasks/`

Save your own work in:

```text
practice/work/
```

# Using A CLI Agent

If you use a CLI agent such as Codex CLI or Claude Code, open the terminal in only the copied work folder assigned for the task.

For example, to start the Day 2 practice:

```bash
cd practice/work/01_clean-and-visualize
```

Then start your agent from that folder.

Do not start the agent from the full course repository unless the task explicitly asks for that. Do not let agents edit course-owned folders such as `modules/` or `practice/tasks/`. A smaller working folder gives the agent less irrelevant context and reduces the chance of editing the wrong files.

# Quarto Project Roots

Some practice work folders include a local `_quarto.yml` file.

Quarto uses the nearest `_quarto.yml` to decide where a project starts. This helps local paths such as `data/county_wellness_indicators_long.csv` resolve from the assigned folder.

If you render from a terminal, first move into the assigned folder:

```bash
cd practice/work/01_clean-and-visualize
quarto render 01_data-cleaning.qmd
```

Note that if you want to use Quarto, you will need to install Quarto manually if you are not using RStudio or Positron.

# Quick Check

Before moving on, confirm:

- `git --version` works.
- You cloned `ai-tools-2026`.
- `index.html` opens.
- `git pull --ff-only` works, or `source("updater.R")` works if you use R.
- You know where `practice/work/` is.
- If using a CLI agent, you know how to start it from the assigned folder under `practice/work/`.
